coredata.dc:description: Microspherical ZnO composites were prepared by introducing carbon dots (CDs) in situ by a one-step hydrothermal method. The effects of precursor and calcination treatments on the microstructure and morphology, compositional features and triethylamine (TEA) detection were carefully investigated. The results show that the addition of excess urea promotes the formation of CDs, the produced UC-ZnO composite presents microspheres with relatively smooth surface after calcination, and the retained CDs form heterojunctions with ZnO. TC-ZnO composite produced by adding excess trisodium citrate displays bowl-shaped depressions on the surface. After calcination, the porous structures with large specific surface area and carbon incorporation are achieved. The two composites show improved gas-sensitive response and reduced detection limit for TEA. The sensitivity for 100 ppm TEA at the optimal operating temperature of 350°C is 357 for UC-ZnO and 526 for TC-ZnO sensor, which are 21% and 80% higher than that of pure ZnO, respectively. The TC-ZnO sensor possesses an excellent low detection limit of 32 ppb. The introduction of CDs effectively modulates the morphology and interface state of ZnO microspheres, and improves the gas-sensitive performance.

head.ce:title: Morphology modulation of ZnO microspheres and improvement of triethylamine detection performance by introducing carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microspherical ZnO composites were prepared by introducing carbon dots (CDs) in situ by a one-step hydrothermal method. The effects of precursor and calcination treatments on the microstructure and morphology, compositional features and triethylamine (TEA) detection were carefully investigated. The results show that the addition of excess urea promotes the formation of CDs, the produced UC-ZnO composite presents microspheres with relatively smooth surface after calcination, and the retained CDs form heterojunctions with ZnO. TC-ZnO composite produced by adding excess trisodium citrate displays bowl-shaped depressions on the surface. After calcination, the porous structures with large specific surface area and carbon incorporation are achieved. The two composites show improved gas-sensitive response and reduced detection limit for TEA. The sensitivity for 100 ppm TEA at the optimal operating temperature of 350°C is 357 for UC-ZnO and 526 for TC-ZnO sensor, which are 21% and 80% higher than that of pure ZnO, respectively. The TC-ZnO sensor possesses an excellent low detection limit of 32 ppb. The introduction of CDs effectively modulates the morphology and interface state of ZnO microspheres, and improves the gas-sensitive performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Triethylamine (TEA) is widely used as solvents, catalysts and raw materials in organic synthesis industry. As a volatile organic compound gas (VOC), high concentrations of TEA could threaten seriously to health, such as causing damages to the respiratory system, liver and kidneys [1,2]. Therefore, rapid and accurate detection of TEA is urgently needed. Researchers have been being committed to developing highly sensitive and selective sensors for the efficient detection of TEA. Metal oxide semiconductor (MOS) sensors have aroused significant interest in the field of gas detection due to the advantages of low cost, less power consumption, easy fabrication, and compatibility with various fabrication processes [3–5]. Currently, a variety of MOS gas-sensitive materials are developed for TEA detection, including SnO2, ZnO, TiO2, In2O3, MoO3, MgO, Al2O3 and WO3 [6–12]. Among them, ZnO is taken as the one of the ideal candidates due to its direct band gap (3.37 eV), large exciton binding energy (60 meV) and high electron mobility (0.022 m2/V·s) [13–15]. The gas-sensitizing properties of ZnO are affected by many factors, including the morphology, heterojunction and element doping and so on.

Optimizing the structure and morphology has been a conventional strategy to induce features in MOS to improve the detection properties [16]. In recent years, there has been significant research on ZnO nanomaterials with diverse morphologies, including nanowires, nanorods, nanoribbons, nanoflowers, nanotubes, and more [17–20]. Xu et al. reported that the three-dimensional nanoflower-shaped ZnO exhibits superior gas-sensing response and selectivity towards TEA [21]. Li et al. successfully synthesized ZnO micropyramids by molten salt method, and found that the sensor showed a response of 45–50 ppm TEA at 300°C with a detection limit of 2 ppm [22]. It is considered that the large specific surface area in the three-dimensional structure could promote the reaction between adsorbed oxygen ions and gas molecules. Park et al. reported the ZnO microspheres with a hierarchical porous structure through the calcination of zinc precursor. The porous microspherical ZnO structures demonstrates high selectivity and sensitivity towards NOx gases by increasing gas adsorption and diffusion channels, providing more active sites and higher charge transfer capacity [23]. The results suggest that the three-dimensional spherical ZnO materials display high gas response owing to their large specific surface area and a modifiable microstructure.

Morphology modifiers have been successfully applied in regulating the structure of ZnO. Sun et al. employed a hydrothermal method to synthesize zinc carbonate wrapped with carbon spheres, and then high-temperature treatment was performed to harvest ZnO microspheres with hollow eggshell structure [24]. The unique bilayer structure provides more active sites for the reaction between oxygen ions and TEA gas. Carbon dots (CDs), due to the ultra-small size and abundant surface functional groups, have been served as morphology modulators for MOS to control their nucleation and growth. Hou et al. proposed a novel mechanism involving the in-situ growth of MOS induced by CDs, where MxOy/CDs composites with pomegranate-like porous structure were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method using CDs dispersed in ethanol as dopant [25]. Furthermore, CDs exhibit the modulatable electronic transport and tend to form heterojunction with MOS to improve gas-sensitive performance [26]. Yu et al. prepared ZnO/CDs composites by co-mingled mechanical milling, and remarked that the composites possess higher carrier concentration and improved gas response properties [27,28]. Although some progress has been made in ZnO/CDs composites, few has been reported on the modification of ZnO surface structure by introducing CDs in situ. The different precursors and treatment process definitely affects the microstructure and interfacial properties of the composites. More efforts are needed to elucidate the effect of these factors on the gas detection mechanism.

In this work, pure ZnO and the composite materials are synthesized by way of a one-step hydrothermal method, and CDs are introduced in situ through controlling the amount of urea and trisodium citrate. The morphology of the ZnO composites can be regulated by varying the CDs introduction coupling with calcination treatment. The effect of CDs on the structure evolution of the composites is carefully investigated with respect to the morphology, microstructure and composition. Furthermore, the gas selectivity and sensitivity towards TEA are systematically studied, and the response mechanism is discussed.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate ((Zn(NO3)2·6H2O)≥99.0%) was purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd. Urea (CH4N2O ≥ 99.0%) and trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O ≥ 99.0%) were achieved from Tianjin Comeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of microspherical ZnO and the composite materials
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO was synthesized by dissolving 1.78494 g of zinc nitrate, 0.48048 g of urea, and 0.17646g of trisodium citrate in 80 ml of deionized water. The mixture was stirred magnetically for 30 min until a transparent solution was formed. The obtained mixed solution was then transferred to a 100 mL capacity autoclave with Teflon-lined stainless steel, and the temperature was maintained at 120°C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the autoclave was washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol for 3 times each, and then centrifuged to achieve a white precipitate. The precipitate was dried in air at 80°C for 10 h to get the zinc hydroxide carbonate precursors. Subsequently, the precursors were ground and subjected to calcination at 500°C for 2 h under vacuum to obtain pure ZnO microspheres.

Furthermore, use urea as the carbon precursor to prepare the UC-ZnO precursor, with the amount of 0.7207 g, and use trisodium citrate to prepare TC-ZnO precursor with the amount of 0.2941 g. At last, the UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO compositest were obtained after the calcination of the above precursors.

Phase analysis was performed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker-D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation). The structure, morphology and composition were analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Carl Zeiss, Merlin Compact), energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-JEM 2100F). Elemental composition and energy level structure analysis were performed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi). The absorption spectrum was tested with UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan), and the fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer (F97, Prism Technology). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted by thermogravimetric analyzer (Netzsch STA 499 F3). Surface area and pore size distribution were achieved through five-point Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) (Micromeritics Triatar 3020 device).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and measurement of gas sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared sample was mixed with deionized water and milled to yield a homogeneous paste. Then the paste was coated on the Ag/Pd substrate to form a sensing film. The gas-sensitive substrate was aged at 275°C for 48 h before gas sensitivity testing.

The gas-sensing performance of the sensor was tested by a static gas-sensing test system (WS-60A (Winson Co. Ltd., China)). The gas response is calculated as R a/R g, where R a and R g represent the resistance of the sensor in the air and in target gas, respectively. Response time is defined as the duration to reach 90% of its maximum response, and recovery time is the time required to achieve a recovery of 90%.

Fig. 1 (a) demonstrates the XRD patterns of pure ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO precursors. The similar diffraction peaks of them depicted could perfectly index to monoclinic hydrozincite, where the strong diffraction peaks at 27.9°, 31.0°, 33.1° and 59.5° are corresponded to (020), (220), (021), and (023) planes of monoclinic hydrozincite (JCPDS 11–0287). The uncalcined materials primarily exist in the form of zinc hydroxide carbonate [23,29]. Additionally, TC-ZnO precursor exhibits a weak diffraction peak at a diffraction angle of 25.2°, aligning with the graphite (100) crystal plane, which indicates the presence of carbon in the matrix of ZnO [30]. Fig. 1(b) displays the XRD patterns of the three samples after calcination, and it is can be seen that the structure has changed greatly. The pure ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO exhibit the similar diffraction peaks and are consistent with hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO (JCPDS 36–1451). The strong and sharp diffraction peaks at 31.7°, 34.4° and 36.2° are observed, indicating good crystallinity. During the calcination process under vacuum conditions, zinc hydrogen carbonate undergoes decomposition and leads to a complete phase transformation to ZnO with evaporation of CO2 and H2O [31], which reduces the carbon content. For UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO, there is no carbon-related diffraction peaks are observed, which indicates that the calcination reduces the content of carbon.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM images of all the samples before and after calcination. It is clearly that ZnO maintains the microspherical structure after doping with CDs and undergoing high-temperature calcination, while the size and surface morphology significantly change. Uncalcined pure ZnO precursors presents as 12 μm-sized microspheres consisting of vertically grown porous nanosheets, as shown in Fig. 2(a). After calcination, the size of the microspheres reduces to 4 μm, and dense nanorods are distributed on the surface (see Fig. 2(d)). This transformation may be attributed to the decomposition of the precursor and evaporation of chemical impurities, such as water and carbonate, which leads to high supersaturation of ZnO intermediates and impels the nucleation. As a result, the particle size of microspheres reduces and the surfaces are condensed from nanosheets to nanorods. As illustrated in Fig. 2(b), the uncalcined UC-ZnO precursor presents as 11.8 μm-sized microspheres, assembled by the stacked nanosheets who cross each other and share endpoints. After calcination, the UC-ZnO transforms into a solid sphere with a diameter of about 5 μm (Fig. 2(e)). The sphere consists of closely arranged nanoparticles with a relatively smooth surface, and some irregular nanorods and nanoparticles scattered sporadically. This phenomenon is commonly observed during the calcination of organic-inorganic hybrid materials [32,33], where the organic components decompose and produce the inorganic residues in various forms.

Fig. 2(c) depicts FE-SEM images of TC-ZnO precursor and the microspheres with a diameter of about 9.5 μm. The surfaces are rough and is characteristic of the hemispherical bowl-shaped depression with irregular nanoparticles attached. After calcination, the microsphere size decreases to 6.4 μm, and further the depressions become deeper and the porosity is increased (Fig. 2(f)). The initial nanoparticles attached on the surface transform into nanorods or nanospheres. The FE-SEM results reveal that pure ZnO and the composites microspheres experience a reduction in size after calcination. Compared with pure ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO composites are smaller in size before calcination and become larger after, implying that the introduction of the carbon material slows down the calcination-induced decrease in ZnO size. Notably, the different proportions of precursors result in significant differences in the surface morphology of the composites, and excess trisodium citrate promotes the formation of porous ZnO composite. The EDS elemental scan images of the calcined samples are shown in Fig. S1. The three samples are composed of Zn, O and C, and the carbon content has decreased significantly compared to the ones before calcination. During calcination, more defects might be introduced as the decomposition takes away some carbon.

The calcined pure ZnO and composite materials were characterized by TEM to gain further insight into the microstructure, as shown in Fig. 3 . The microspheres of all three samples consiste of 50–100 nm nanoparticles, which interconnecte with each other to form a sheet-like structure dotted with pores (see Fig. 3(a,d,g)). It is clearly that pure ZnO displays the larger rectangular nanosheet in size, while the nearly circular nanosheets with smaller size are observed in UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO. More importantly, the dispersed CDs with a size of around 7 nm can be observed at the edge of ZnO nanosheet in the image of UC-ZnO (Fig. 3(e)). The HRTEM images in Fig. 3(c,f,i) show resolved fringes with a separation of 0.26 nm, corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO crystals [34]. Particularly, UC-ZnO displays an additional lattice fringe with a plane spacing of 0.21 nm, which might correspond to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [35,36]. These results indicate that CDs is confirmed to exist in UC-ZnO composite.

The TG-DSC curves of the three precursors are depicted in Fig. 4 . As the temperature gets rise to approximately 240°C, all samples exhibit a slight weight loss. Subsequently, a drastic weight loss of around 23.4 wt% occurs in the range of 240–350°C, which is possibly associated with the evaporation of H2O and CO2. All three samples show a corresponding strong exothermic peak at 283.7°C in Fig. 4(b). Compared to ZnO and TC-ZnO, UC-ZnO has a relatively small exothermic rate, which may be resulted from the fact that the CDs are encapsulated and not completely decomposed. Notably, two additional small exothermic peaks at 383.6°C and 457.0°C are observed for TC-ZnO, and further weight loss is found in this temperature range, with total weight loss of 31.2 wt%. The results may be attributed to the structure transformation of CDs and crystal growth of ZnO [23], which simultaneously lead to the formation of pores.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the FTIR spectra of ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO precursors. For all samples, the absorption peak at 3669.5 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of the O–H group has been found. Additionally, two absorption peaks at 1392 cm−1 and 1559 cm−1 representing the asymmetric vibration absorption of CO3 2−, implies that the samples primarily consisted of zinc hydroxide carbonate prior to calcination. Weak absorption peaks of CC at 830 cm−1 and Zn–O stretching vibration at 461.08 cm−1 are observed [37]. As compared to ZnO, UC-ZnO displays a C–O vibration absorption peak at 935 cm−1. TC-ZnO possesses two additional absorption peaks at 1235 cm−1 and 1061 cm−1, corresponding to the C–H and C–O bending vibration, respectively. An enhanced C–H absorption at 3000 cm−1 is detected for TC-ZnO. After calcination, the three samples display a stretching vibration peak of the Zn–O bond at 461.08 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 5(b). During the calcination process, the volatilization of H2O and CO2 greatly reduces the C and O content, thus no characteristic absorption peaks are observed with long wave number for all the samples. In comparison to pure ZnO and TC-ZnO, UC-ZnO exhibiting a relatively strong vibrational absorption peaks of CC at 885.1 cm−1, indicates that some CDs might be retained in UC-ZnO after calcination.

To further investigate the composition, the optical properties were tested and analyzed. Fig. 6 (a) shows the fluorescence spectrum of the uncalcined samples under the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. No fluorescence is observed in pure ZnO precursor, while UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO display the blue fluorescence with emission peak at 414 nm and 473 nm, respectively. In order to clarify the source of fluorescence, two kinds of CDs were synthesized using urea and trisodium citrate respectively as the carbon precursors without zinc source. Their fluorescence emissions are observed at 430 nm and 448 nm (see Fig. S2). Hence, it is believed that the fluorescence in the composite materials mainly originates from the corresponding CDs [38,39]. The fluorescence spectra of the calcined samples are compared in Fig. 6(b). Both ZnO and TC-ZnO present an intrinsic emission from ZnO at 387 nm [40]. However, UC-ZnO displays a fluorescence emission from CDs at 422 nm, which is red-shifted by 8 nm compared to the uncalcined sample. This result further confirms that CDs are preserved. For TC-ZnO sample, the CDs undergo the decomposition at high temperatures due to their attachment to the ZnO surface, resulting in the fluorescence disappearance from CDs.

The absorption spectra reveal that none of the uncalcined samples show absorption peaks (as shown in Fig. S3), which is attributed to the predominance of zinc hydroxide carbonate in the matrix. Furthermore, the calcined ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO exhibit the absorption peaks at 369.4 nm, 380.5 nm and 373.5 nm, consisting with the band edge absorption of ZnO [41]. The results suggest that the high-temperature calcination promotes the formation of wurtzite structure ZnO, while the addition of carbon materials leads to the red shift of absorption peak. The bandgap energy (E g) may be extracted from their (ahv)2 versus hv plots and is shown in inset of Fig. 6(b). The values of ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO are calculated to be 3.08 eV, 2.42 eV and 2.95 eV, respectively. The decrease in bandgap energy of composites is normally correlated with the rise in lattice defects [42,43], implying that TC-ZnO has more defects than that of pure ZnO. In this work, UC-ZnO exhibits the lowest bandgap energy, which might be contributed from the addition of urea to synthesize ZnO [44].

The porosity and surface area of the samples using BET and BJH were characterized and shown in Fig. 7 . The calcination increase the specific surface area, such as from 5.14 to 10.58 m2/g for ZnO, from 3.98 to 8.38 m2/g for UC-ZnO, and from 5.85 to 9.62 m2/g for TC-ZnO. After calcination, the pore sizes of all the samples increase and fall in the mesopore range. The values are listed in Table 1 . The most significant change in pore size is observed for TC-ZnO, with an increase from 11.1 to 12.5 nm. A large surface area and a high amount of nanoscale pores is beneficial for the diffusion of target gases and the electrons exchange during surface reactions [45].

The composition and chemical states of the calcined pure ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO were analyzed by XPS. Fig. 8 (a–c) with the full spectrum indicate the clear signals of Zn, O and C. The C1s spectra of three samples are shown in Fig. 8(d–f). Characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.9 eV are assigned to the C–C bond, C–O bond, and carboxyl carbon (O–CO) [28,29], respectively. The order C–O content is Pure ZnO (16.68%) < UC-ZnO (24.91%) < TC-ZnO (33.57%), suggesting increased carbon content in composites [43]. The O1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three sub-peaks with respective binding energies of 530.44 eV, 531.61 eV and 532.35 eV, corresponding to the lattice oxygen (OL), the oxygen vacancy (Ov) and the surface hydroxyl oxygen (OOH), respectively [46]. The order Ov content is Pure ZnO (19.57%) < UC-ZnO (23.74%) < TC-ZnO (32.05%), revealing the existence of more oxygen vacancy defects in TC-ZnO. Oxygen vacancy is expected to be the active sites for sensing reactions, where they can act as electron donors and then increase the concentration of adsorbed oxygen and facilitate the reactions with target gas [47,48]. In Fig. 8(g, h, i), the Zn energy level of pure ZnO microspheres exhibits two fitting peaks located at approximately 1044.69 eV and 1021.69 eV, corresponding to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively. In the other two samples, the Zn 2p fitting peak shifts towards higher binding energy. This shift might be attributed to the changes in the chemical environment and microstructure caused by the introduction of carbon [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth mechanism of the composite microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned, composites with different morphology were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method by regulating the amount of urea and trisodium citrate, and the formation process is analyzed in Fig. 9 . During the reaction, urea is decomposed and produce OH− and CO3 2− ions. OH− ions firstly combine with Zn2+ to form zinc hydroxide Zn(OH)2, which gradually grows to form microspheres. At the same time, CO3 2− ions proceed to react with Zn(OH)2 to form Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2, which attaches to the Zn(OH)2 spheres and resembles flakes [23]. As the reaction progresses, Zn(OH)2 gradually decreases and is completely converted to Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2, and the citrate ions (C5H7O5COO−) control the surface morphology of the microspheres to form the sheet structure. Subsequently, the high temperature calcination process induces the decomposition of Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2, leading to the formation of mesoporous ZnO microspheres as H2O and CO2 evaporate, i.e. pure ZnO sample.

When the excess urea is added in the reaction solution, UC-ZnO is achieved. The growth process is analyzed as follows. In parallel with the formed Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2 sheets, the excess urea provides amino groups and reacts directly with trisodium citrate to form CDs [50–52]. Their existence has been confirmed by above results of TEM (Fig. 3) and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 6), and the CDs would attach to the surface of the nanosheets. Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2 sheets appear to cross each other and share endpoints, and the tips appear to be partially glued. During the high-temperature calcination process, Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2 is decomposed to form ZnO, and the nanosheets merge with each other, leading to the destruction of the lamellar structure. Some CDs might be encapsulated within the microspheres, resulting in the formation of ZnO/CDs composites, i.e. UC-ZnO sample.

When the amount of trisodium citrate is increased in the reaction, TC-ZnO sample is gained. The principle of the reaction is as follows. According to the principle of ionic reaction equilibrium, the excess citrate ions (C5H7O5COO−) in solution can inhibit the further transfer from Zn(OH)2 to Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2 [53]. The solid sphere of Zn(OH)2 is formed and displays the reduced size compared to the pure lamellar microspheres obtained by complete dissolution of Zn(OH)2. As the reaction proceeds, Zn5(OH)6(CO3)2 might aggregate to nucleate and then grows into small spheres, and attaches on the surface of solid Zn(OH)2 sphere. Accompanied by the growth of small balls and the regulatory of C5H7O5COO−, they might detach from the solid Zn(OH)2 spheres, eventually leaving the bowl-shaped depressions on the surface. At the same time, a small amount of urea provides amino groups (NH2 −) which would react with citrate ions to form CDs, and then they would attach to the surface of the spheres to form the complexes. During the high-temperature vacuum calcination process, ZnO nanospheres are generated as H2O and CO2 evaporated, and the CDs would be decomposed because of the lack of encapsulated. Meanwhile, the bowl-shaped depressions are transformed into more and deeper pore depressions, finally ZnO microspheres containing a large number of pore structures are obtained. These processes are aligned with the SEM results.

Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the sensitivity curves of ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO sensors to TEA (100 ppm) in the range of operating temperature from 250°C to 400°C. The response of three sensors displays an “increase−maximum−decrease” mode with the increase of working temperature. Pure ZnO exhibits a maximum sensitivity of 305 at 375°C. The response sensitivity of the composites is higher than that of pure ZnO, 357 for UC-ZnO and 526 for TC-ZnO, with an optimum operating temperature of 350°C. The composite sensors possess the slightly reduced optimal working temperature due to the increased activated energy, which is crucial to overcome the activation energy barrier and stimulate the reaction on the surface [48]. The resistance variation curves of the three sensors to different concentrations of TEA (10–100 ppm) at 350°C operating temperature are shown in Fig. 10(b). The initial resistance in the air (Ra) of the obtained samples are 2.35 × 106 Ω, 3.41 × 106 Ω and 7.13 × 106 Ω for ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO, respectively. The larger Ra is mainly due to the influence of heterojunctions and porous structures in UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO. It can be seen that the resistance of three sensors gradually decreases with the increase of TEA concentration. The response values increase with increasing gas concentration. At the same concentration, the response order is pure ZnO < UC-ZnO < TC-ZnO, suggesting that the introduction of carbon material improves the performance of ZnO sensors. Many factors affect the sensor sensitivity, such as specific surface area, the interface state, the number of active sites, etc. Although the specific surface area of UC-ZnO is relatively small (8.38 m2/g), the gas-sensitivity is better than that of pure ZnO due to the formation of heterojunction between CDs and ZnO [27]. For TC-ZnO, a large number of mesopores and the carbon doping in the microspheres increase the gas adsorption active sites, resulting in a significant improved gas response. The amount of urea and trisodium citrate have been investigated. We suggest that urea with 0.7207 g and trisodium citrate with 0.2941 g are the best amounts to gain optimal gas sensing properties (see in Fig. S4).

Fig. 10(c) shows the fitted curves for the response value of three sensors with increasing concentration of TEA from 10 ppm to 100 ppm. The fitting slopes for ZnO, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO sensors are calculated to be 2.86, 3.71 and 5.48, respectively. The response values of TC-ZnO display the largest increase. The theoretical limit of detection was estimated by using standard method (3σ/S), where σ represented the standard deviation (SD) and S represented the slope of sensing curve. The detection limits are 118 ppb for ZnO and 63 ppb for UC-ZnO. It is worth noting that the response of TC-ZnO toward 10 ppm TEA is up to 28, and the detection limits is as low as 32 ppb, which provides an application prospect for detecting low concentration TEA. Fig. 10(d) presents the response sensitivity to different gases of the same concentration (100 ppm) at 350°C operating temperature. Seven volatile organic gases, including acetone, triethylamine, ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, ammonia, toluene and methanol were selected for testing. The responses of three sensors to TEA are much higher than that of other gases, indicating excellent selectivity. Notably, the selectivity of TC-ZnO is improved compared with that of pure ZnO and UC-ZnO.

Fig. 11 demonstrates the dynamic response-recovery curves of ZnO, UC-ZnO, and TC-ZnO sensors to TEA (100 ppm). The gas response times are 61 s, 54 s, and 53 s, while the recovery times are 51 s, 33 s, and 34 s, respectively. Compared to the pure ZnO sensor, the other two sensors exhibit faster recovery times, with a reduction of approximately 35%. Fig. 12 illustrates the repeatability and stability at the operating temperature of 350°C. The sensitivity of three sensors maintains good consistency in 5 cycles of gas response to 100 ppm TEA. The long-term stability was evaluated over a period of 12 months (Fig. 12(b)). After being placed for 30 days, the gas-sensitive response of the pure ZnO sensor only decreases by 1%, while UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO sensors decrease by 6% and 5%, respectively. Subsequently, the sensitivity exhibits small fluctuations. After 360 days, the sensitivity of three sensors is only reduced by 14%, 11% and 12% respectively, meaning good long-term gas-sensing stability. In order to estimate the sensing performance of TC-ZnO sensor, a comprehensive comparison of key metrics (operating temperature, response and detection limit) was performed and listed in Table 2 [16,22,24,49,54–58]. The gas sensing performance is significantly influenced by the structure and morphology. It is noteworthy that the porous TC-ZnO sensor prepared in this study demonstrates the certain advantages gas-sensitive response and detection limit compared with ZnO nanostructure sensors reported in previous works. In addition, the present sensor has also excellent stability, which is an essential criterion in the practical application. Overall, the TC-ZnO-based sensors has an outstanding potential as a sensing device for TEA detection.

The theory of space-charge layer mode has been widely accepted and applied to explain the mechanism of MOS based sensors [16]. The gas sensing performance depends on the adsorption/desorption of gas molecules as well as the chemical and physical reaction on the material surface. The prepared materials exhibit quite different surface morphology, surface area and oxygen vacancy concentration due to the introduction of CDs. These differences seem to be responsible for the significant disparity in the absorption of oxygen and reaction between gas molecules, which play an important role in the progression of sensing response [43]. As we know, ZnO is an n-type semiconductor with electronic conductivity, when it is exposed to the air, oxygen molecules adsorbed onto the ZnO surface could capture the free electrons in the conductive band of ZnO to form oxygen ions (O2−, O−, O2 −). As a result, the ZnO material loses electrons and forms an electron depletion layer on the surface, leading to the reduction in electron concentration and increase in resistance. When ZnO is exposed to TEA, the gas molecules react with the adsorbed oxygen ions to form N2, H2O and CO2. At the same time, the previously trapped electrons are released back into the conductive band of ZnO, causing a thin depletion layer and the increase in the carrier quantity, then the resistance would be decreased [59]. The above reaction can be expressed as shown in the following formulas 1-6:

Compared to the pure ZnO sensor, UC-ZnO and TC-ZnO sensors exhibit an increased gas sensitivity. However, the composites possess the smaller specific surface area than pure ZnO. Except for surface area, gas sensing properties of MOS are significantly affected by interface state, which is decided by microstructure, element-doping and heterojunction [60,61]. At the interface between MOS/CDs composite materials, heterojunction structures are easily formed due to different bandgaps [25,48,62]. For UC-ZnO, such improvement can be attributed to the presence of CDs and the formation of heterojunction with ZnO as shown in Fig. 13 (a). The TEM and HRTEM images show that CDs attach to the edge of ZnO nanoparticles and form the connection interface. Fig. 13 (c) illustrates the energy band diagrams and the formation of heterojunction. Since the work function of ZnO (5.3 eV) is lower than that of CDs (5.56 eV), electrons would flow from ZnO to CDs to reach Fermi energy level equilibrium. The heterojunction is conducive to change more O2 molecules to chemically oxygen (O2 −, O−, and O2−) by capturing electrons, forming widened electron depletion region [63], which creates a favorable environment for the reaction of TEA molecules with adsorbed oxygen ions. CDs possesses excellent activity of edges on the surface, which facilitates the adsorption of gas molecules and accelerates the reaction [64]. More electrons are released back into the ZnO conduction band during gas detection, which improving the gas-sensitive performance.

TC-ZnO exhibits better gas sensitivity than UC-ZnO. It is considered that there may be two factors as following. Firstly, TC-ZnO microspheres exhibit many pores with different sizes and the larger specific surface area (9.62 m2/g) than UC-ZnO (8.38 m2/g), which plays a key role in the gas-sensing properties of MOS due to providing reaction channels for gas adsorption [65]. The TEM and HRTEM images of TC-ZnO show the porous structures, and the electron transfer process can be obviously accelerated [66]. The classic mean free paths of TEA molecules is approximately 36 nm, it is larger than the pore size of TC-ZnO microspheres (12.5 nm). Therefore, the transport of the gas molecules is governed by Knudsen diffusion [12,67]. According to Knudsen diffusion theory, when TC-ZnO is exposed to TEA, the gas molecules would be more likely to collide with the pore wall, and the reaction rate between TEA and adsorbed oxygen ions would be increased. As a result, the trapped electrons are rapidly released back into the conduction band, increasing the carrier concentration and decreasing the resistance [63,68]. Secondly, gas sensing properties of MOS are significantly affected by surface defects [20]. TC-ZnO possesses the largest content of oxygen vacancies (32.05%) due to the doping of carbon atoms in the ZnO matrix after calcination, which has been proved by UV–vis and XPS in Figs. 7 and 9. More carbon-related defects in TC-ZnO provide abundant surface-active sites for adsorption, reaction, and desorption of TEA molecules [69]. The superior TEA sensing performance of TC-ZnO is attributed to synergistic effects of the porous structures and abundant surface defects.

In summary, when the other two sensors are exposed to the air, more O2 molecules are absorbed and react with free electrons from ZnO to form more oxygen ion adsorption groups. As a result, a thicker electron depletion layer occurs, and the resistance value increases. Once the sensor is exposed to TEA gas, more adsorbed oxygen ions would react with the target gas. During this process, the carrier concentration increases further than the pristine corresponding sensor, resulting in a significant decrease of the sensor resistance.

In this work, pure ZnO and two ZnO/C composites doped with carbon dots were prepared using a friendly one-pot hydrothermal method. Various characterization techniques such as XRD, XPS, SEM and TEM were employed to analyze the materials. Interestingly, the micro-morphology is successfully modulated only by varying the precursor ratio within the calcination process. The UC-ZnO microspheres, prepared with urea as the carbon precursor, are composed of nanoparticles with a relatively smooth surface, and some CDs are effectively coated. A heterojunction between ZnO and CDs is formed, which increases the number of active sites and then improves the TEA gas sensing response (357) and reduces the detection limit (63 ppb). TC-ZnO, synthesized using sodium citrate as the carbon precursor, exhibits deep and dense porous structure and the doping of carbon. Compared to pure ZnO and UC-ZnO, the TC-ZnO composite demonstrates the higher gas-sensitive response (526) and the lower detection limit (32 ppb) for TEA. The enhanced performance is mainly attributed to the combination of gas transport and Knudsen diffusion caused by porous structure and oxygen-related defects resulting from carbon doping and calcination. This work provides an idea for C-doped MOS nanomaterials to improve gas sensing performance through the synergistic effect of morphology control and defect modulation.
